facts.t: The United States marks its Bicentennial with fireworks and tall ships filling New York Harbor.
Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak found Apple in a California garage.
Nadia Comaneci scores the first perfect 10 in Olympic gymnastics history at the Montreal Games.
California's Chateau Montelena Chardonnay stuns France by winning the blind Judgment of Paris wine tasting.
Legionnaires' disease is first identified after a deadly outbreak at a Philadelphia hotel convention.
Star Wars premieres and becomes an instant cultural phenomenon.
Elvis Presley dies at Graceland at age 42.
A 25-hour citywide blackout during a heat wave plunges New York City into chaos and looting.
Smokey and the Bandit turns bootlegged Coors beer into a box-office smash.
The Apple II, one of the first mass-produced personal computers, goes on sale.
The Camp David Accords are signed, a landmark Middle East peace framework brokered by President Carter.
President Carter signs a law legalizing homebrewing beer for personal use nationwide.
Muhammad Ali beats Leon Spinks to become the first man to win the heavyweight title three times.
Garfield the comic strip debuts and soon becomes one of the most widely syndicated in the world.
Grease, starring John Travolta and Olivia Newton-John, becomes a smash hit at the box office.
Militants storm the US Embassy in Tehran, starting the 444-day Iran hostage crisis.
A partial meltdown at Three Mile Island becomes the worst nuclear accident in US history.
ESPN launches as the first all-sports cable network.
The Pittsburgh Pirates win the World Series behind their anthem, We Are Family.
Pink Floyd releases The Wall, one of the best-selling albums of all time.
The US hockey team's Miracle on Ice upset of the Soviets electrifies the Lake Placid Olympics.
Mount St. Helens erupts in Washington state, killing 57 people and reshaping the landscape.
Ted Turner launches CNN, the first 24-hour cable news network.
Mothers Against Drunk Driving is founded after a hit-and-run drunk-driving death.
The Who Shot J.R. cliffhanger on Dallas becomes a nationwide television obsession.
President Reagan is shot and wounded by John Hinckley Jr. outside a Washington hotel.
MTV launches, kicking off with the video for Video Killed the Radio Star.
Sandra Day O'Connor becomes the first woman to sit on the US Supreme Court.
IBM releases its first Personal Computer, helping set the standard for the industry.
Space Shuttle Columbia makes its first flight, opening NASA's shuttle program.
The Vietnam Veterans Memorial is dedicated on the National Mall in Washington, D.C.
Seven people die in the Chicago Tylenol murders, leading to the tamper-proof packaging used today.
E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial becomes the highest-grossing film of its era.
Michael Jackson releases Thriller, which goes on to become the best-selling album ever.
Coca-Cola launches Diet Coke, its first new cola brand since 1886.
Sally Ride becomes the first American woman in space aboard Space Shuttle Challenger.
The series finale of M*A*S*H becomes the most-watched television broadcast in US history at the time.
Cabbage Patch Kids dolls spark a holiday shopping frenzy and nationwide shortages.
Return of the Jedi closes out the original Star Wars trilogy in theaters.
A truck bomb attack on the Marine barracks in Beirut kills 241 US service members.
The Los Angeles Olympics open, with the US winning a record medal haul amid a Soviet-led boycott.
Apple debuts the Macintosh with its famous 1984 Super Bowl commercial.
Congress passes a law pushing every state to raise its legal drinking age to 21.
Ghostbusters becomes one of the biggest box-office hits of the year.
US health officials identify HIV as the cause of AIDS.
Coca-Cola replaces its formula with New Coke, triggering a backlash so fierce the original recipe returns within months.
Back to the Future becomes the top-grossing film of the year.
We Are the World, recorded by USA for Africa, tops the charts and raises millions for famine relief.
Live Aid concerts in Philadelphia and London raise millions for Ethiopian famine relief.
The historic Route 66 is officially decommissioned as a US highway.
Space Shuttle Challenger breaks apart 73 seconds after launch, killing all seven crew members.
The Statue of Liberty's centennial is marked with a massive fireworks display in New York Harbor.
The Oprah Winfrey Show goes into national syndication, launching Oprah to superstardom.
The Iran-Contra affair becomes public, roiling the Reagan administration.
Halley's Comet makes its once-every-76-years pass by Earth.
Black Monday sees the Dow Jones plunge 22.6 percent in a single day, its worst one-day drop ever.
Aretha Franklin becomes the first woman inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.
Dirty Dancing, filmed on a shoestring budget, becomes a surprise box-office smash.
The FDA approves Prozac, ushering in a new class of antidepressants.
The Simpsons debut as animated shorts on The Tracey Ullman Show.
Pan Am Flight 103 is bombed over Lockerbie, Scotland, killing 270 people, most of them American.
Massive wildfires sweep through Yellowstone National Park, burning more than a third of it.
George H. W. Bush is elected the 41st President of the United States.
Die Hard, starring Bruce Willis, redefines the modern action movie.
The Phantom of the Opera opens on Broadway, later becoming its longest-running show.
The fall of the Berlin Wall marks a turning point in the end of the Cold War.
The Exxon Valdez spills roughly 11 million gallons of crude oil into Alaska's Prince William Sound.
Nintendo launches the Game Boy handheld console in North America.
Tim Burton's Batman, starring Michael Keaton, becomes the summer's blockbuster.
The Seinfeld pilot airs, eventually growing into one of TV's most acclaimed sitcoms.
President Bush signs the Americans with Disabilities Act into law.
NASA launches the Hubble Space Telescope aboard Space Shuttle Discovery.
Home Alone becomes the highest-grossing live-action comedy of its time.
East and West Germany reunify, formally ending the Cold War division.
Milli Vanilli is stripped of its Grammy after it's revealed the duo never sang on their album.
A US-led coalition launches Operation Desert Storm, liberating Kuwait from Iraqi occupation.
The Soviet Union officially dissolves, ending nearly seven decades of communist rule.
Nirvana releases Nevermind, propelling grunge into the mainstream.
Magic Johnson announces he has contracted HIV and is retiring from the NBA.
A bystander video of the Rodney King beating by LAPD officers ignites a national reckoning over police brutality.
The Los Angeles riots erupt after officers are acquitted in the Rodney King beating, killing more than 60 people.
Bill Clinton is elected the 42nd President of the United States.
Hurricane Andrew devastates South Florida as one of the costliest hurricanes in US history.
The Mall of America, the largest shopping mall in the US, opens in Minnesota.
The US Dream Team, led by Michael Jordan and Magic Johnson, dominates Olympic basketball in Barcelona.
A truck bomb detonates in the World Trade Center's parking garage, killing six people.
The 51-day standoff at the Branch Davidian compound in Waco, Texas, ends in a deadly fire.
Jurassic Park, powered by groundbreaking CGI, becomes a global blockbuster.
Ty Inc. launches Beanie Babies, which soon become a nationwide collecting craze.
The Mosaic web browser is released, helping bring the internet to the mainstream public.
O. J. Simpson's slow-speed Ford Bronco chase is watched live by nearly 95 million Americans.
Figure skater Nancy Kerrigan is clubbed in the knee in an attack tied to rival Tonya Harding's camp.
A players' strike forces the cancellation of the World Series for the first time in 90 years.
The Northridge earthquake strikes the Los Angeles area, killing dozens and causing billions in damage.
Woodstock '94 draws hundreds of thousands to a mud-soaked 25th-anniversary festival in upstate New York.
A truck bomb destroys the Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in Oklahoma City, killing 168 people.
Jeff Bezos launches Amazon.com as an online bookstore.
Pierre Omidyar starts what becomes eBay as an online auction site called AuctionWeb.
Toy Story premieres as the first entirely computer-animated feature film.
The DVD format is jointly developed as the successor to VHS and laserdisc.
Atlanta hosts the Summer Olympics, marred by a deadly bombing in Centennial Olympic Park.
TWA Flight 800 explodes and crashes off Long Island, killing all 230 people aboard.
President Clinton signs sweeping welfare reform legislation into law.
Tickle Me Elmo becomes the sold-out, must-have toy of the holiday season.
NASA announces possible evidence of ancient microbial life in a Martian meteorite found in Antarctica.
NASA's Mars Pathfinder lands and deploys the Sojourner rover, the first wheeled vehicle on another planet.
Titanic opens in theaters and goes on to become the highest-grossing film ever at the time.
Tiger Woods wins the Masters at 21, the tournament's youngest-ever champion.
Members of the Heaven's Gate cult are found dead in a mass suicide timed to the Hale-Bopp comet.
IBM's Deep Blue defeats world chess champion Garry Kasparov in a full match.
The House of Representatives impeaches President Bill Clinton over the Lewinsky scandal.
Larry Page and Sergey Brin found Google.
Mark McGwire and Sammy Sosa's home run chase captivates baseball fans all season.
The FDA approves Viagra, the first oral pill for erectile dysfunction.
Titanic ties the record by winning 11 Academy Awards, including Best Picture.
Two students kill 12 classmates and a teacher at Columbine High School before taking their own lives.
Shawn Fanning launches Napster, upending the music industry with peer-to-peer file sharing.
The Matrix redefines action filmmaking with its bullet-time visual effects.
Woodstock '99 descends into fires and unrest, tarnishing the festival's legacy.
Nationwide Y2K fears mount over whether computers will fail when the date rolls to 2000.
The disputed Bush v. Gore presidential election is settled by a Supreme Court ruling more than a month after Election Day.
The final original Peanuts comic strip runs the day after creator Charles Schulz's death.
Scientists announce completion of a working draft of the human genome.
The New York Yankees beat the New York Mets in the first Subway Series World Series since 1956.
Sony launches the PlayStation 2 in the US, which goes on to become the best-selling console ever.
